Title: Brassinosteroid signaling in plants – a paradigm for steroid hormone signaling?

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant growth and development; male fertility; xylem differentiation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: overexpression suppression analysis in bri1 extracellular domain mutants
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression suppresses multiple defects of bri1 extracellular-domain mutants only when functional BRI1 kinase domain and active BRs are present
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BRS1 encodes a likely secreted carboxypeptidase-like protein. 2. BRS1 overexpression can suppress defects of certain bri1 extracellular-domain mutants. 3. It may modulate the BRI1 signaling pathway via protein processing.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Possible BR-signaling modulator, but utility remains uncertain because direct substrate and mechanism are unresolved
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Likely acts indirectly; substrates are unknown and may include steroid-binding proteins

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: type 2C protein phosphatase (PP2C), kinase-associated protein phosphatase
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant growth and development; male fertility; xylem differentiation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: in vitro interaction with BRI1 kinase domain; comparative evidence from CLV1 pathway genetics summarized in review
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Potential negative regulator of BRI1-mediated brassinosteroid signaling
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. KAPP is a PP2C that potentially interacts with the BRI1 kinase domain in vitro. 2. It is proposed to oppose BRI1 kinase activity. 3. By analogy with other RLK pathways, it may negatively regulate BR signaling.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to fine-tune BR signaling, though evidence in BR pathway remains provisional
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Role in BR signaling is suggested but not fully demonstrated in this review

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Leucine-rich repeat receptor-like kinase (LRR-RLK), plasma-membrane Ser/Thr receptor kinase
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant growth and development; male fertility; xylem differentiation; stimulation of cell elongation
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: ligand perception and signal transduction via receptor autophosphorylation
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: mediates genomic brassinosteroid responses; induces expression of BR-responsive genes such as XETs indirectly
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: major brassinosteroid receptor required for BR sensitivity
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: genetic mutant analysis; positional cloning; GFP localization; domain-swap assay with XA21; radioligand [3H]-BL binding; immunoprecipitation; phosphorylation analysis; MALDI-MS autophosphorylation site mapping
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Promoter-driven GFP observed in hypocotyl cells
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Hypocotyl cell surface fluorescence in transgenic plants
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Possible dimerization with LRR-RLKs not definitively established
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss-of-function causes brassinosteroid insensitivity, dwarfism and reduced fertility-associated BR-defective phenotypes; functional receptor restores/mediates BR responses
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BRI1 is the major brassinosteroid receptor in Arabidopsis thaliana. 2. Its extracellular 70-amino-acid island is required for brassinolide binding. 3. Its intracellular kinase domain is an active Ser/Thr kinase that autophosphorylates after BR perception.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating BRI1 can tune plant stature and BR responsiveness, but strong loss-of-function causes severe dwarfism and developmental defects
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced growth and fertility in BR-insensitive mutants
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper is a review; BRI1 is a primary summarized BR signaling gene rather than a newly experimentally discovered gene in this article

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: BRI1 homolog, leucine-rich repeat receptor-like kinase
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Controls growth and development through BR sensitivity
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: rice homolog mediating BR sensitivity, indicating conserved RLK-based steroid signaling
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: mutant/functional homolog evidence summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OsBRI1 is the rice homolog of BRI1. 2. It mediates BR sensitivity in rice. 3. BR signaling via RLKs is suggested to be a common theme in plants.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for modifying rice architecture and BR responsiveness
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely growth defects if signaling is severely impaired
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned in the perspectives section as conserved BR receptor signaling
